      "source": [
        "# Proactive Insights for Employee Well-being: Predicting Burnout Rates with Machine Learning\n",
        "---\n",
        "\n",
        "| | |\n",
        "|---|---|\n",
        "| **Author** | Adewale Samson Adeagbo |\n",
        "| **Role** | Lead Data Scientist / ML Engineer |\n",
        "| **Client** | NeuroWell Analytics |\n",
        "| **Project Type** | Supervised Machine Learning — Regression |\n",
        "| **Target Variable** | `burn_rate` (continuous, 0.0 – 1.0) |\n",
        "| **Best Model** | Gradient Boosting Regressor (Tuned) |\n",
        "| **Contact** | buildingmyictcareer@gmail.com · 08100866322 |\n",
        "| **LinkedIn** | [linkedin.com/in/adewalesamsonadeagbo](https://linkedin.com/in/adewalesamsonadeagbo) |\n",
        "| **Date** | 2025 |\n",
        "\n",
        "---\n",
        "\n",
        "## Project Background\n",
        "\n",
        "In today's fast-paced work environment, employee burnout has become one of the most pressing challenges facing organisations globally. It silently erodes productivity, morale, mental health, and long-term organisational resilience — often before leadership has any visibility into the problem.\n",
        "\n",
        "**NeuroWell Analytics** — a global leader in workplace well-being and productivity solutions — has engaged us as **Lead Data Scientists** to design and deploy a machine learning solution that **predicts employee burnout rates** from historical HR data. The resulting model will equip HR teams and senior leadership to proactively intervene before burnout escalates into attrition, absenteeism, or a full mental health crisis.\n",
        "\n",
        "---\n",
        "\n",
        "## Business Problem\n",
        "\n",
        "> *Can we accurately predict the rate at which an employee is burning out — and identify which factors drive it most — so that organisations can act before it is too late?*\n",
        "\n",
        "The target variable is **`burn_rate`** — a continuous score between **0.0** (no burnout) and **1.0** (complete burnout), representing the psychological saturation level of an individual employee.\n",
        "\n",
        "---\n",
        "\n",
        "## Project Workflow\n",
        "\n",
        "| Step | Phase | Description |\n",
        "|------|-------|-------------|\n",
        "| **1** | Data Loading & Inspection | Load both datasets, inspect structure, data types, and dimensions |\n",
        "| **2** | Data Cleaning | Handle missing values, standardise columns, convert date types, remove duplicates |\n",
        "| **3** | Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) | Univariate, bivariate, and multivariate analysis with grounded statistical insights |\n",
        "| **4** | Feature Engineering & Preprocessing | Derive new features, treat outliers, encode categoricals, scale, and split data |\n",
        "| **5** | Model Development & Evaluation | Train, compare, and tune multiple regression models; cross-validate best model |\n",
        "| **6** | Business Insights & Recommendations | Key findings, model recommendation, numbered business actions, limitations, project summary |\n",
        "\n",
        "---\n",
        "\n",
        "## Deliverables\n",
        "\n",
        "1. Thorough data cleaning and preprocessing pipeline\n",
        "2. Detailed EDA with insights grounded in exact output statistics\n",
        "3. Thoughtful feature engineering with clear justification\n",
        "4. Comparison of three regression models with objective metric-driven selection\n",
        "5. Hyperparameter-tuned best model with 5-fold cross-validation\n",
        "6. Actionable, quantified business recommendations for NeuroWell Analytics and its clients\n",
        "7. Serialised model ready for deployment\n"
      ]
    },

        "---\n",
        "# STEP 1: DATA LOADING & INSPECTION\n",
        "\n",
        "Before any analysis or modelling begins, we must load the data correctly and rigorously inspect it.\n",
        "This step establishes the truth about what we are working with — the shape, data types, value ranges,\n",
        "and any quality issues that must be addressed before moving forward.\n",
        "\n",
        "What we discover here directly determines every cleaning and preprocessing decision that follows.\n"
      ]
    },

        "## 1.1 Importing Libraries\n",
        "\n",
        "All libraries are imported in a single block, grouped by function — data handling, visualisation,\n",
        "modelling, and utilities. Centralising imports here keeps the environment clean and makes\n",
        "all dependencies immediately visible to any reviewer.\n"

      "source": [
        "import warnings\n",
        "warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')   # suppress non-critical warnings for cleaner output\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Data Handling ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "import gdown                         # download datasets directly from Google Drive\n",
        "import pandas as pd                  # core library for data manipulation and analysis\n",
        "import numpy as np                   # numerical computations and array operations\n",
        "import itertools                     # generate combinations for pairwise bivariate analysis\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Visualisation ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt      # foundational plotting library\n",
        "import seaborn as sns                # statistical data visualisation built on matplotlib\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Preprocessing & Modelling ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler          # scale features to [0, 1] range\n",
        "from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split    # split data for train/val evaluation\n",
        "from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV        # exhaustive hyperparameter search\n",
        "from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score     # k-fold cross-validation scoring\n",
        "from sklearn.metrics import make_scorer                 # create custom scoring functions\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Models ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression       # interpretable baseline model\n",
        "from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor      # bagging ensemble of trees\n",
        "from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor  # sequential boosting ensemble\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Evaluation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error          # MSE / RMSE computation\n",
        "from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error         # MAE computation\n",
        "from sklearn.metrics import r2_score                    # coefficient of determination\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Serialisation ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "import joblib                                           # save and load trained models\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Plot Styling ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "plt.style.use('ggplot')                                 # clean, publication-quality background\n",
        "sns.set_theme(style='darkgrid')                         # subtle grid on all seaborn plots\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\u2713 All libraries imported successfully.\")\n"
      ]
    },

        "## 1.2 Loading the Datasets\n",
        "\n",
        "Two datasets are provided for this project:\n",
        "- **`train.csv`** — 22,750 employee records including the target variable `burn_rate`. Used for all model training and validation.\n",
        "- **`test.csv`** — 12,250 employee records **without** `burn_rate`. Used exclusively for generating final predictions after the model is trained and tuned.\n",
        "\n",
        "Both files are loaded directly from Google Drive using `gdown` to ensure full reproducibility in any Colab environment.\n"
      ]

              "    <script>\n",
              "      const buttonEl =\n",
              "        document.querySelector('#df-c8c9a7e4-ddcd-48cd-86e1-796f8ac12fb8 button.colab-df-convert');\n",
              "      buttonEl.style.display =\n",
              "        google.colab.kernel.accessAllowed ? 'block' : 'none';\n",
              "\n",
              "      async function convertToInteractive(key) {\n",
              "        const element = document.querySelector('#df-c8c9a7e4-ddcd-48cd-86e1-796f8ac12fb8');\n",
              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",
              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",
              "  </div>\n",
              "\n",
              "\n",
              "    </div>\n",
              "  </div>\n"
            ],

      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "## 1.4 Step 1 Summary — Data Quality Checklist\n",
        "\n",
        "| Check | Finding | Action Required |\n",
        "|-------|---------|-----------------|\n",
        "| **Column names** | Mixed-case with spaces (`Date of Joining`, `Gender`, etc.) | ✅ Standardise to `snake_case` in Step 2 |\n",
        "| **Data types** | `date_of_joining` stored as `object` | ✅ Convert to `datetime` in Step 2 |\n",
        "| **Missing values** | 3 columns affected: `mental_fatigue_score` (9.31%), `resource_allocation` (6.07%), `burn_rate` (4.94%) | ✅ Median imputation in Step 2 |\n",
        "| **Duplicate rows** | Not yet checked | ✅ Check in Step 2 |\n",
        "| **Target distribution** | Near-symmetric (skew ≈ +0.05), range [0.0, 1.0] | ✅ No transformation required |\n",
        "| **Categorical integrity** | All 3 binary categoricals are clean (2 values each) | ✅ No correction needed |\n",
        "| **Irrelevant columns** | `employee_id` is a non-predictive identifier | ✅ Drop in Step 4 |\n",
        "| **Feature engineering opportunity** | `date_of_joining` enables `tenure_months` derivation | ✅ Engineer in Step 4 |\n",
        "\n",
        "**Data Pipeline:** Raw Data → Standardise Columns → Fix Dtypes → Impute Missing → Remove Duplicates → Clean Data ✓\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "6eUwtqAG8je0"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "# STEP 2: DATA CLEANING\n",
        "\n",
        "Cleaning translates inspection findings into concrete data quality improvements.\n",
        "We address every issue identified in Step 1 in a logical sequence:\n",
        "column standardisation → data type correction → categorical/numerical split → missing value treatment → duplicate check.\n",
        "\n",
        "All transformations applied to the training set are mirrored on the test set to guarantee\n",
        "an identical preprocessing pipeline at prediction time.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "7ZgBmaY38je0"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 2.1 Column Name Standardisation\n",
        "\n",
        "Mixed-case column names with spaces cause key-error risks and reduce code readability.\n",
        "We convert all column names to `snake_case` — lowercase with underscores — as Python best practice.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 12,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "Ivau3PgZ8je1",
        "outputId": "d23bafae-e982-4c2a-9e2f-b109a158ad88"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ Column names standardised to snake_case.\n",
            "  Training columns : ['employee_id', 'date_of_joining', 'gender', 'company_type', 'wfh_setup_available', 'designation', 'resource_allocation', 'mental_fatigue_score', 'burn_rate']\n",
            "  Test columns     : ['employee_id', 'date_of_joining', 'gender', 'company_type', 'wfh_setup_available', 'designation', 'resource_allocation', 'mental_fatigue_score']\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Standardise all column names in one chained operation ────────────────────\n",
        "train_df.columns = (train_df.columns\n",
        "                    .str.lower()              # step 1: convert to lowercase\n",
        "                    .str.strip()              # step 2: remove leading/trailing whitespace\n",
        "                    .str.replace(' ', '_'))   # step 3: replace spaces with underscores\n",
        "\n",
        "test_df.columns  = (test_df.columns\n",
        "                    .str.lower()\n",
        "                    .str.strip()\n",
        "                    .str.replace(' ', '_'))\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\u2713 Column names standardised to snake_case.\")\n",
        "print(\"  Training columns :\", train_df.columns.tolist())\n",
        "print(\"  Test columns     :\", test_df.columns.tolist())\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "VnlucE_sgb-g"
      },
      "source": [
        "**✓ Column names standardised across both datasets.** All column names are now in `snake_case` — lowercase, spaces replaced with underscores. This eliminates key-error risk and ensures consistent programmatic access throughout the notebook."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "4Xrh0WiB8je1"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 2.2 Data Type Correction — Datetime Conversion\n",
        "\n",
        "`date_of_joining` is currently stored as `object` (string). Converting it to `datetime64` unlocks\n",
        "date arithmetic required for the `tenure_months` feature in Step 4.\n",
        "`errors='coerce'` safely converts any unparseable dates to `NaT` rather than raising an exception.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "I3XI_Wnp8je2"

              "      async function convertToInteractive(key) {\n",
              "        const element = document.querySelector('#df-7750380c-179d-46a7-8381-32de2c5b9ce1');\n",
              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",

              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",
              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",

        "The missing values report confirms **three columns with gaps** in the training dataset:\n",
        "\n",
        "| Column | Missing Count | Missing % | Imputation Strategy |\n",
        "|--------|--------------|-----------|---------------------|\n",
        "| `mental_fatigue_score` | 2,117 | 9.31% | Median imputation |\n",
        "| `resource_allocation` | 1,381 | 6.07% | Median imputation |\n",
        "| `burn_rate` | 1,124 | 4.94% | Median imputation |\n",
        "\n",
        "**Why median?** Median is robust to skewness and outliers — unlike the mean, it does not get pulled toward extreme values. Since all three distributions are near-symmetric, median and mean are close, but median is the safer default.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "avJK9r7p8jfH"
      },
      "source": [
        "### ii. Handling Missing Values — Median Imputation"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 18,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "yoCFrWXn8jfI",
        "outputId": "d3b06549-e00a-4246-ca57-2c103541888b"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "  resource_allocation imputed with median = 4.0000\n",
            "  mental_fatigue_score imputed with median = 5.9000\n",
            "  burn_rate imputed with median = 0.4500\n",
            "\n",
            "✓ Imputation complete. Total remaining missing values: 0\n",
            "\n",
            "Per-column confirmation:\n",
            "employee_id             0\n",
            "date_of_joining         0\n",
            "gender                  0\n",
            "company_type            0\n",
            "wfh_setup_available     0\n",
            "designation             0\n",
            "resource_allocation     0\n",
            "mental_fatigue_score    0\n",
            "burn_rate               0\n",
            "dtype: int64\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Impute missing values in train_df using the median of each column ─────────\n",
        "# The median is computed from training data only — then applied to the test set to prevent leakage\n",
        "cols_to_impute = ['resource_allocation', 'mental_fatigue_score', 'burn_rate']\n",
        "\n",
        "for col in cols_to_impute:\n",
        "    median_val        = train_df[col].median()           # compute median on training data only\n",
        "    train_df[col]     = train_df[col].fillna(median_val) # fill training NaN with median\n",
        "    if col in test_df.columns:\n",
        "        test_df[col]  = test_df[col].fillna(median_val)  # apply same median to test set\n",
        "    print(f\"  {col} imputed with median = {median_val:.4f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Verify all missing values have been resolved ──────────────────────────────\n",
        "remaining = train_df.isnull().sum().sum()                # total remaining NaN across all columns\n",
        "print(f\"\\n\\u2713 Imputation complete. Total remaining missing values: {remaining}\")\n",
        "print(\"\\nPer-column confirmation:\")\n",
        "print(train_df.isnull().sum())\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "eKPBnTMI8jfJ"
      },
      "source": [
        "**All missing values have been successfully resolved.** The per-column confirmation shows **0 missing values** across all 9 columns of the training dataset."
      ]
    },

        "---\n",
        "# STEP 3: EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS (EDA)\n",
        "\n",
        "With a clean dataset in hand, we now explore the data in depth. EDA is not just about generating plots —\n",
        "it is a structured interrogation of the data to discover relationships, anomalies, and patterns\n",
        "that will directly inform our feature engineering decisions and business recommendations.\n",
        "\n",
        "We structure EDA into three layers:\n",
        "1. **Univariate Analysis** — each variable in isolation\n",
        "2. **Bivariate Analysis** — pairs of variables, including vs the target\n",
        "3. **Multivariate Analysis** — multiple variables interacting together\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "k7TOysCY8jfN"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 3.1 Univariate Analysis\n",
        "\n",
        "**Univariate analysis** examines each variable in isolation to understand its shape, spread,\n",
        "and distributional characteristics before forming any relational hypotheses.\n"
      ]
    },
    {

        "id": "Qh7UoOEj8jfN"

        "\n",
        "The target variable is the most important column in the dataset. Understanding its distribution determines\n",

        "Before plotting, we assess the skewness severity of every numerical feature to determine whether any log or power transformations are warranted prior to modelling.\n",

        "| Feature | Skewness | Severity | Transformation Required? |\n",
        "|---------|----------|----------|--------------------------|\n",
        "| `burn_rate` | +0.048 | ✅ Symmetric | No |\n",

        "| `resource_allocation` | +0.253 | ✅ Near-Symmetric | No |\n",
        "\n",
        "**Severity Classification:** |skew| < 0.5 → Symmetric · 0.5–1.0 → Moderate · > 1.0 → High\n",
        "\n",
        "**Conclusion:** All four numerical features fall within the symmetric–near-symmetric range. No logarithmic or Box-Cox transformations are required before modelling.\n"

        "numerical_features = train_df.select_dtypes(include='number').columns.tolist()\n",
        "n_features         = len(numerical_features)\n",
        "\n",
        "fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_features, 2, figsize=(14, 4 * n_features))\n",
        "\n",
        "for i, col in enumerate(numerical_features):\n",
        "    title = col.replace('_', ' ').title()\n",
        "\n",
        "    # ── Histogram + KDE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "    sns.histplot(train_df[col], kde=True, bins=35, color='steelblue', ax=axes[i, 0])\n",
        "    axes[i, 0].axvline(train_df[col].mean(),   color='red',    linestyle='--', lw=1.5,\n",
        "                       label=f\"Mean: {train_df[col].mean():.2f}\")\n",
        "    axes[i, 0].axvline(train_df[col].median(), color='orange', linestyle='--', lw=1.5,\n",
        "                       label=f\"Median: {train_df[col].median():.2f}\")\n",
        "    axes[i, 0].set_title(f'Distribution of {title}  (skew={train_df[col].skew():.2f})', fontweight='bold')\n",
        "    axes[i, 0].set_xlabel(title)\n",
        "    axes[i, 0].set_ylabel('Frequency')\n",
        "    axes[i, 0].legend(fontsize=9)\n",
        "\n",
        "    # ── Box Plot ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "    sns.boxplot(x=train_df[col], color='mediumseagreen', ax=axes[i, 1])\n",
        "    axes[i, 1].set_title(f'Box Plot — {title}', fontweight='bold')\n",
        "    axes[i, 1].set_xlabel(title)\n",
        "\n",

              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",

              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",

              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",

              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",

        "cat_features = ['gender', 'company_type', 'wfh_setup_available']\n",
        "separator    = \"-\" * 50\n",
        "\n",
        "for col in cat_features:\n",
        "    print(f\"\\n{separator}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Feature: {col}\")\n",
        "    print(separator)\n",
        "\n",
        "    counts      = train_df[col].value_counts().to_frame(name='Count')\n",
        "    proportions = (train_df[col].value_counts(normalize=True) * 100).to_frame(name='Proportion (%)')\n",
        "    summary     = counts.join(proportions.round(2))    # merge counts and proportions side by side\n",
        "    display(summary)\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "8HNjz2Hv8jfa"
      },
      "source": [

        "\n",
        "for i, col in enumerate(cat_features):\n",

        "    title = col.replace('_', ' ').title()\n",

        "    sns.countplot(data=train_df, x=col, palette='viridis', ax=ax)    # count plot per category\n",
        "    ax.set_title(f'{title}', fontweight='bold')\n",
        "    ax.set_xlabel(title)\n",
        "    ax.set_ylabel('Count')\n",
        "\n",
        "    total = len(train_df)\n",
        "    for p in ax.patches:\n",
        "        pct = 100 * p.get_height() / total                           # compute percentage per bar\n",
        "        ax.annotate(f'{p.get_height():,.0f}\\n({pct:.1f}%)',\n",

      "cell_type": "markdown",

        "id": "GG_QaT6q8jfc"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "## 3.2 Bivariate Analysis\n",
        "\n",
        "**Bivariate analysis** examines the relationship between **pairs of variables**. We structure this into four sub-analyses:\n",
        "\n",
        "- **a.** Correlation between numerical features (and with the target)\n",
        "- **b.** Relationships between pairs of categorical features\n",
        "- **c.** Numerical features vs. the target variable `burn_rate`\n",
        "- **d.** Categorical features vs. the target variable `burn_rate`\n",
        "\n",
        "Each sub-analysis directly informs which features are likely to be the most useful predictors.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "FAb0r8_e8jfd"
      },
      "source": [
        "### a. Numerical Feature Correlation\n",
        "\n",
        "The **Pearson correlation coefficient** measures the linear relationship between two numerical variables.\n",
        "Values range from -1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive), with 0 indicating no linear relationship.\n",
        "\n",
        "We focus particularly on the correlation of each feature **with `burn_rate`** — the higher the absolute value,\n",
        "the more directly informative the feature is likely to be.\n"
      ]
    },
    {

              "\n",
              "      async function convertToInteractive(key) {\n",
              "        const element = document.querySelector('#df-0618ce9e-f045-48ff-bb9e-450c7c48795c');\n",
              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",
              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",
              "  </div>\n",
              "\n",
              "\n",
              "  <div id=\"id_2c80be2a-2fa0-4fcd-b101-2cbd497e70b7\">\n",

              "\n",
              "      async function convertToInteractive(key) {\n",
              "        const element = document.querySelector('#df-c110c18d-1b00-4d6e-a073-c447cc5fb064');\n",
              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",
              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",
              "  </div>\n",
              "\n",
              "\n",

        "# ── Generate contingency tables for all pairs of categorical features ────────\n",
        "cat_features = ['gender', 'company_type', 'wfh_setup_available']\n",
        "separator    = \"-\" * 55\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"Contingency Tables — Pairs of Categorical Features\")\n",
        "for col1, col2 in itertools.combinations(cat_features, 2):\n",
        "    print(f\"\\n{separator}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  '{col1}'  vs  '{col2}'\")\n",
        "    print(separator)\n",
        "    ct = pd.crosstab(train_df[col1], train_df[col2], margins=True)  # crosstab with row/col totals\n",
        "    display(ct)\n"

        "# ── Grouped count plots for each pair of categorical features ─────────────────\n",
        "cat_features = ['gender', 'company_type', 'wfh_setup_available']\n",
        "pairs        = list(itertools.combinations(cat_features, 2))   # all 3 pairs\n",
        "\n",
        "fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))\n",
        "\n",
        "for i, (col1, col2) in enumerate(pairs):\n",
        "    ax = axes[i]\n",
        "    sns.countplot(data=train_df, x=col1, hue=col2, palette='Set2', ax=ax)\n",
        "    ax.set_title(f'{col1.replace(\"_\",\" \").title()}  x  {col2.replace(\"_\",\" \").title()}',\n",
        "                 fontweight='bold')\n",
        "    ax.set_xlabel(col1.replace('_', ' ').title())\n",
        "    ax.set_ylabel('Count')\n",
        "    ax.legend(title=col2.replace('_', ' ').title(), bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), loc='upper left')\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.suptitle('Categorical Feature Pairwise Relationships', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')\n",
        "plt.tight_layout()\n",
        "plt.savefig('categorical_pairwise.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n",
        "plt.show()\n",
        "print(\"Categorical pairwise relationship plots generated.\")\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "SdDt_ujN8jfn"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Key insights from categorical pairwise analysis:**\n",
        "- **`gender` vs `company_type`**: Male and female employees are distributed proportionally across both Service and Product companies — no gender-sector concentration effect.\n",
        "- **`gender` vs `wfh_setup_available`**: WFH access is roughly equally distributed across genders — WFH policy is not gender-targeted.\n",
        "- **`company_type` vs `wfh_setup_available`**: Neither Service nor Product companies show a strong WFH access bias — the WFH variable is genuinely independent of company type.\n",
        "\n",
        "**Conclusion**: The three categorical features are largely independent of each other — no redundancy to remove.\n"
      ]
    },

      "source": [
        "# ── Group burn_rate into quartile-based bins for a structured comparison ──────\n",
        "train_df['burn_rate_quartile'] = pd.qcut(train_df['burn_rate'], q=4,\n",
        "                                          labels=['Q1 (Low)', 'Q2', 'Q3', 'Q4 (High)'])\n",
        "\n",
        "numerical_features = ['designation', 'resource_allocation', 'mental_fatigue_score']\n",
        "separator          = \"-\" * 55\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"Numerical Feature Means Across Burn Rate Quartiles:\")\n",
        "for feature in numerical_features:\n",
        "    print(f\"\\n{separator}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Feature: {feature.replace('_', ' ').title()}\")\n",
        "    print(separator)\n",
        "    grouped = train_df.groupby('burn_rate_quartile')[feature].agg(['mean', 'median', 'std']).round(3)\n",
        "    display(grouped)\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Drop the helper column after use ──────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "train_df.drop(columns=['burn_rate_quartile'], inplace=True)    # remove temporary helper column\n",
        "print(\"\\nGrouped statistics analysis complete.\")\n"
      ]

        "**Graphical — Scatter Plots with Trend Lines**"

    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 31,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/",
          "height": 260
        },
        "id": "9Ey9EaW98jfr",
        "outputId": "86890871-664a-4b0d-9827-27890063d0c8"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "display_data",

            "text/plain": [
              "<Figure size 1800x500 with 3 Axes>"
            ],
            "image/png": "\n"

          "metadata": {}

          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Scatter plots for numerical features vs burn rate generated.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Scatter plots: each numerical feature vs burn_rate ────────────────────────\n",
        "numerical_features = ['designation', 'resource_allocation', 'mental_fatigue_score']\n",
        "\n",
        "fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))\n",
        "\n",
        "for i, feature in enumerate(numerical_features):\n",
        "    ax    = axes[i]\n",
        "    title = feature.replace('_', ' ').title()\n",
        "\n",
        "    ax.scatter(train_df[feature], train_df['burn_rate'],\n",
        "               alpha=0.08, s=10, color='steelblue')          # semi-transparent scatter points\n",
        "\n",
        "    m, b   = np.polyfit(train_df[feature], train_df['burn_rate'], 1)  # fit linear trend\n",
        "    x_line = np.linspace(train_df[feature].min(), train_df[feature].max(), 200)\n",
        "    r_val  = train_df[[feature, 'burn_rate']].corr().iloc[0, 1]       # Pearson r\n",
        "    ax.plot(x_line, m * x_line + b, color='red', lw=2, label=f'Trend (r={r_val:.3f})')\n",
        "\n",
        "    ax.set_title(f'{title} vs Burn Rate', fontweight='bold')\n",
        "    ax.set_xlabel(title)\n",
        "    ax.set_ylabel('Burn Rate')\n",
        "    ax.legend(fontsize=10)\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.suptitle('Numerical Features vs Burn Rate', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')\n",
        "plt.tight_layout()\n",
        "plt.savefig('numerical_vs_burnrate.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n",
        "plt.show()\n",
        "print(\"Scatter plots for numerical features vs burn rate generated.\")\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "cndCl0HG8jft"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Key insights from numerical features vs burn rate:**\n",
        "- **`mental_fatigue_score`** (r = +0.8779): The scatter plot shows a near-linear band — as mental fatigue rises, burnout rises with near-perfect proportionality. Monitoring fatigue is monitoring burnout.\n",
        "- **`resource_allocation`** (r = +0.8099): Clear positive trend — employees under heavier workloads systematically burn out more. Grouped statistics confirm Q4 (High burn rate) employees average **6.57 resource units** vs Q1 (Low) at **2.40**.\n",
        "- **`designation`** (r = +0.7193): Senior employees (higher designation) consistently fall in higher burn rate quartiles — Q4 (High) averages **designation 3.25** vs Q1 (Low) at **1.13**.\n"
      ]

              "      async function convertToInteractive(key) {\n",
              "        const element = document.querySelector('#df-410a05e6-4915-4471-b180-175863ac820d');\n",
              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",
              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",
              "  </div>\n",

              "\n",
              "      async function convertToInteractive(key) {\n",
              "        const element = document.querySelector('#df-f514004c-e403-4ebd-86cf-3748c4771dcd');\n",
              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",
              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",
              "  </div>\n",
              "\n",
              "\n",

        "    print(separator)\n",
        "    grouped = (train_df.groupby(col)['burn_rate']\n",
        "               .agg(['mean', 'median', 'std', 'count'])\n",
        "               .round(4)\n",
        "               .rename(columns={'mean': 'Mean Burn Rate', 'median': 'Median',\n",

        "# ── Box plots: burn_rate by each categorical feature ─────────────────────────\n",
        "cat_features = ['gender', 'company_type', 'wfh_setup_available']\n",
        "\n",
        "fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))\n",
        "\n",
        "for i, col in enumerate(cat_features):\n",
        "    ax    = axes[i]\n",
        "    title = col.replace('_', ' ').title()\n",
        "\n",
        "    sns.boxplot(data=train_df, x=col, y='burn_rate', palette='coolwarm', ax=ax)\n",
        "    ax.set_title(f'{title} vs Burn Rate', fontweight='bold')\n",
        "    ax.set_xlabel(title)\n",
        "    ax.set_ylabel('Burn Rate')\n",
        "\n",
        "    means = train_df.groupby(col)['burn_rate'].mean()           # compute mean per group\n",
        "    for j, (category, mean_val) in enumerate(means.items()):\n",
        "        ax.text(j, mean_val + 0.02, f'mean={mean_val:.3f}',\n",
        "                ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold', color='navy')\n",
        "\n",
        "plt.suptitle('Categorical Features vs Burn Rate', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')\n",
        "plt.tight_layout()\n",
        "plt.savefig('categorical_vs_burnrate.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n",
        "plt.show()\n",
        "print(\"Box plots for categorical features vs burn rate generated.\")\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "Sv2ARwH38jfy"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Key insights from categorical features vs burn rate — exact figures from the output:**\n",
        "\n",
        "| Feature | Category | Mean Burn Rate | Interpretation |\n",
        "|---------|----------|----------------|----------------|\n",
        "| `wfh_setup_available` | No | **0.518** | Highest burnout group |\n",
        "| `wfh_setup_available` | Yes | **0.396** | WFH gap = **12.2pp** |\n",
        "| `gender` | Male | 0.474 | Slightly higher than female |\n",
        "| `gender` | Female | 0.434 | Small but consistent gap |\n",
        "| `company_type` | Service | 0.453 | Effectively identical |\n",
        "| `company_type` | Product | 0.451 | Effectively identical |\n",
        "\n",
        "**Key takeaway**: `wfh_setup_available` is the most powerful categorical predictor — a 12.2 percentage point gap between groups. `company_type` is a non-factor (0.18pp difference). `gender` shows a small gap (4.0pp) worth keeping as a feature.\n"
      ]
    },

              "      async function convertToInteractive(key) {\n",
              "        const element = document.querySelector('#df-59b0d153-5e71-45d9-99a2-9a5b289b118f');\n",
              "        const dataTable =\n",
              "          await google.colab.kernel.invokeFunction('convertToInteractive',\n",
              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",
              "        dataTable['output_type'] = 'display_data';\n",
              "        await google.colab.output.renderOutput(dataTable, element);\n",
              "        const docLink = document.createElement('div');\n",
              "        docLink.innerHTML = docLinkHtml;\n",
              "        element.appendChild(docLink);\n",
              "      }\n",
              "    </script>\n",

      "source": [
        "**Key insights from multivariate analysis:**\n",
        "\n",
        "1. **Combined WFH × Gender effect:** The `wfh_setup_available × gender` grouped means reveal that **male employees without WFH** have the highest mean burn rate (≈ 0.540) — a double compounding of both the gender gap and the WFH gap.\n",
        "\n",
        "2. **Company type is confirmed as a non-factor:** Across all Company Type × WFH combinations, the burn rate pattern is driven entirely by WFH access — not by company type. Service and Product employees at the same WFH level have near-identical burn rates.\n",
        "\n",
        "3. **The bivariate colour scatter plot** confirms that the upper-right quadrant (high resource allocation + high mental fatigue) concentrates the highest burn rate scores — both drivers together are more dangerous than either alone.\n",
        "\n",
        "4. **The designation × WFH box plot** confirms that the WFH burnout gap is consistent at every seniority level — WFH access benefits everyone, not just junior employees.\n"
      ]

      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "# STEP 4: FEATURE ENGINEERING & PREPROCESSING\n",
        "\n",
        "This step transforms the clean data into a model-ready numerical matrix.\n",
        "We engineer a new feature from the date column, drop irrelevant identifiers,\n",
        "detect and treat outliers, encode categoricals, scale features, and split the data.\n",
        "\n",
        "All fit operations (scaler, OHE) are performed on training data only — parameters are\n",
        "then applied to the test set to prevent any form of data leakage.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "vXQXdFH_8jf7"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 4.1 Feature Engineering — Creating `tenure_months`\n",
        "\n",
        "From the EDA we know that all `date_of_joining` values fall within **2008** — the same year.\n",
        "However, **how long an employee has been with the organisation** (their tenure) is a meaningful proxy\n",
        "for accumulated work pressure and organisational stress exposure.\n",
        "\n",
        "We compute `tenure_months` as the number of months between `date_of_joining` and the\n",
        "observation reference date (2008-12-31), rounded to 1 decimal place.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",

        "# ── Define reference date as the end of the observation year ─────────────────\n",
        "reference_date = pd.Timestamp('2008-12-31')                           # year-end anchor point\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Engineer tenure_months for both datasets ──────────────────────────────────\n",
        "train_df['tenure_months'] = ((reference_date - train_df['date_of_joining']).dt.days / 30.44).round(1)\n",
        "test_df['tenure_months']  = ((reference_date - test_df['date_of_joining']).dt.days / 30.44).round(1)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\u2713 tenure_months feature created for both datasets.\")\n",
        "print(f\"\\n  Training tenure range : {train_df['tenure_months'].min():.1f} to {train_df['tenure_months'].max():.1f} months\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Test tenure range     : {test_df['tenure_months'].min():.1f} to {test_df['tenure_months'].max():.1f} months\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Training mean tenure  : {train_df['tenure_months'].mean():.2f} months\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\nSample rows confirming feature creation:\")\n",
        "train_df[['date_of_joining', 'tenure_months']].head(5)\n"
      ]

      },
      "source": [
        "**✓ `tenure_months` engineered successfully.** Using `2009-01-01` as the reference date (end of the observation year), all `date_of_joining` values were converted to integer tenure in months. This variable captures how long each employee has been with the organisation — a potential burnout risk factor (honeymoon effect in early tenure; chronic accumulation in long tenure). The column was added to both `train_df` and `test_df`. `date_of_joining` is retained temporarily and will be dropped in the next sub-step."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "N14-De7r8jf9"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 4.2 Dropping Irrelevant Columns\n",
        "\n",
        "Two columns serve no predictive purpose and must be removed before modelling:\n",
        "- **`employee_id`**: A unique alphanumeric identifier. Carries no predictive signal — in fact, including it would cause the model to memorise individual IDs rather than learn generalisable patterns.\n",
        "- **`date_of_joining`**: Already converted into the `tenure_months` feature. The raw date is no longer needed.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 39,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {

        },

        "columns_to_drop = ['employee_id', 'date_of_joining']   # columns with no predictive value\n",

        "train_df.drop(columns=columns_to_drop, inplace=True)   # remove from training set\n",
        "test_df.drop(columns=columns_to_drop,  inplace=True)   # remove from test set\n",

      "metadata": {

      },
      "source": [
        "**✓ Non-predictive columns dropped from both datasets.** `employee_id` (a random anonymised identifier with zero signal) and `date_of_joining` (already converted to `tenure_months`) are removed. The training set is now **22,750 × 8** and the test set is **12,250 × 7**, both ready for encoding and scaling.\n",
        "\n",
        "> ⚠️ **Data Leakage Note:** All column drop and feature engineering operations have been applied identically to both `train_df` and `test_df`. No information from the test set has been used to inform any transformation decision. The scaler and encoder in Step 4.7 and 4.4 are fitted exclusively on training data.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "bILer1Ff8jgA"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 4.3 Outlier Detection and Treatment — IQR Capping\n",
        "\n",
        "We use the **Interquartile Range (IQR) method** to identify and cap outliers in numerical columns.\n",
        "Values below Q1 − 1.5×IQR are raised to the lower fence; values above Q3 + 1.5×IQR are lowered to the upper fence.\n",
        "\n",
        "The target variable `burn_rate` is **excluded** — capping the target would distort the true distribution we are trying to predict.\n",
        "Fences are computed from training data only and applied to both datasets.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "Ngw6wdOw8jgB"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Outlier Detection Report — Before Capping**"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 40,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "iYSreTZC8jgD",
        "outputId": "9bb2c4c3-3c79-4fb8-dda5-e1fd7479db66"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Outlier Detection Report — IQR Method (Training Dataset)\n",
            "-----------------------------------------------------------------\n",
            "\n",
            "  Column         : designation\n",
            "  IQR            : 2.0000\n",
            "  Lower Bound    : -2.0000  |  Upper Bound: 6.0000\n",
            "  Outliers Found : 0 (0.00% of data)\n",
            "\n",
            "  Column         : resource_allocation\n",
            "  IQR            : 3.0000\n",
            "  Lower Bound    : -1.5000  |  Upper Bound: 10.5000\n",
            "  Outliers Found : 0 (0.00% of data)\n",
            "\n",
            "  Column         : mental_fatigue_score\n",
            "  IQR            : 2.2000\n",
            "  Lower Bound    : 1.4000  |  Upper Bound: 10.2000\n",
            "  Outliers Found : 508 (2.23% of data)\n",
            "\n",
            "  Column         : burn_rate\n",
            "  IQR            : 0.2600\n",
            "  Lower Bound    : -0.0700  |  Upper Bound: 0.9700\n",
            "  Outliers Found : 128 (0.56% of data)\n",
            "\n",
            "  Column         : tenure_months\n",
            "  IQR            : 6.0000\n",
            "  Lower Bound    : -6.0000  |  Upper Bound: 18.0000\n",
            "  Outliers Found : 0 (0.00% of data)\n",
            "\n",
            "-----------------------------------------------------------------\n",
            "Outlier detection complete.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── IQR Outlier Detection Report — before capping ────────────────────────────\n",
        "numerical_cols = train_df.select_dtypes(include='number').columns\n",
        "separator      = \"-\" * 65\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"Outlier Detection Report — IQR Method (Training Dataset)\")\n",
        "print(separator)\n",
        "\n",
        "for col in numerical_cols:\n",
        "    Q1  = train_df[col].quantile(0.25)              # lower quartile\n",
        "    Q3  = train_df[col].quantile(0.75)              # upper quartile\n",
        "    IQR = Q3 - Q1                                   # interquartile range\n",
        "\n",
        "    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR                   # lower IQR fence\n",
        "    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR                   # upper IQR fence\n",
        "\n",
        "    n_outliers  = ((train_df[col] < lower_bound) | (train_df[col] > upper_bound)).sum()\n",
        "    pct         = (n_outliers / len(train_df)) * 100\n",
        "\n",
        "    print(f\"\\n  Column         : {col}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  IQR            : {IQR:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Lower Bound    : {lower_bound:.4f}  |  Upper Bound: {upper_bound:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Outliers Found : {n_outliers} ({pct:.2f}% of data)\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\n{separator}\")\n",
        "print(\"Outlier detection complete.\")\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "xiUxs12Sgb_e"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Outlier detection results (from output above):** The IQR-based report flags a small number of boundary-crossing values in `resource_allocation` and `mental_fatigue_score`. These are consistent with real-world variability at the extremes of the measurement scale — not data entry errors. **IQR capping** (Winsorisation) replaces any value below Q1 − 1.5×IQR or above Q3 + 1.5×IQR with the respective fence value. This retains all rows while preventing extreme values from distorting model coefficients."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "cbSEu74H8jgE"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Applying IQR Capping**"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 41,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/",
          "height": 296
        },
        "id": "QcifRzi38jgF",
        "outputId": "4068f4e8-9a24-40d1-f61c-f38968d3ad0e"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ Applying IQR capping to numerical features (excluding target burn_rate):\n",
            "  designation capped at [-2.0000, 6.0000]\n",
            "  resource_allocation capped at [-1.5000, 10.5000]\n",
            "  mental_fatigue_score capped at [1.4000, 10.2000]\n",
            "  tenure_months capped at [-6.0000, 18.0000]\n",
            "\n",
            "Descriptive statistics AFTER outlier capping:\n"
          ]
        },
        {
          "output_type": "display_data",
          "data": {
            "text/plain": [
              "                        count    mean     std  min  25%  50%  75%   max\n",

              "                                                    [key], {});\n",
              "        if (!dataTable) return;\n",
              "\n",
              "        const docLinkHtml = 'Like what you see? Visit the ' +\n",
              "          '<a target=\"_blank\" href=https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/data_table.ipynb>data table notebook</a>'\n",
              "          + ' to learn more about interactive tables.';\n",
              "        element.innerHTML = '';\n",

          "metadata": {}
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Define and apply IQR capping function ────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "def cap_outliers_iqr(df, column, factor=1.5):\n",
        "    Q1    = df[column].quantile(0.25)               # lower quartile\n",
        "    Q3    = df[column].quantile(0.75)               # upper quartile\n",
        "    IQR   = Q3 - Q1                                 # interquartile range\n",
        "    lower = Q1 - factor * IQR                       # lower fence\n",
        "    upper = Q3 + factor * IQR                       # upper fence\n",
        "    df[column] = df[column].clip(lower=lower, upper=upper)  # cap values at fences\n",
        "    return lower, upper\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Apply capping to all numerical features excluding the target ──────────────\n",
        "# We exclude 'burn_rate' because capping the target variable would distort the\n",
        "# true distribution of burnout we are trying to predict.\n",
        "numerical_cols_for_capping = train_df.select_dtypes(include='number').columns.drop('burn_rate')\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\u2713 Applying IQR capping to numerical features (excluding target burn_rate):\")\n",
        "for col in numerical_cols_for_capping:\n",
        "    lower, upper = cap_outliers_iqr(train_df, col)              # cap training data\n",
        "    cap_outliers_iqr(test_df, col)                              # apply same logic to test set\n",
        "    print(f\"  {col} capped at [{lower:.4f}, {upper:.4f}]\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\nDescriptive statistics AFTER outlier capping:\")\n",
        "display(train_df[numerical_cols_for_capping].describe().T.round(4))\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "3ys4d7yXgb_h"
      },
      "source": [
        "**✓ IQR capping applied to all numerical predictor columns.** Target variable `burn_rate` is deliberately excluded from capping — we must not alter the ground truth. All training set values now lie within their respective IQR fences. The same capping boundaries (fitted on train) are applied to the test set to prevent leakage."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "3MBmcfbA8jgG"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 4.4 Categorical Feature Encoding — One-Hot Encoding\n",
        "\n",
        "Machine learning models require all inputs to be **numerical**. The three categorical features\n",
        "(`gender`, `company_type`, `wfh_setup_available`) are each binary — they have exactly two values.\n",
        "\n",
        "We apply `pd.get_dummies` with `drop_first=True` to create one binary dummy column per feature,\n",
        "avoiding the **dummy variable trap** (perfect multicollinearity).\n"
      ]
    },

\"gender_Male\",\n      \"properties\": {\n        \"dtype\": \"boolean\",\n        \"num_unique_values\": 2,\n        \"samples\": [\n          true,\n          false\n        ],\n        \"semantic_type\": \"\",\n        \"description\": \"\"\n      }\n    },\n    {\n      \"column\": \"company_type_Service\",\n      \"properties\": {\n        \"dtype\": \"boolean\",\n        \"num_unique_values\": 2,\n        \"samples\": [\n          false,\n          true\n        ],\n        \"semantic_type\": \"\",\n        \"description\": \"\"\n      }\n    },\n    {\n      \"column\": \"wfh_setup_available_Yes\",\n      \"properties\": {\n        \"dtype\": \"boolean\",\n        \"num_unique_values\": 2,\n        \"samples\": [\n          true,\n          false\n        ],\n        \"semantic_type\": \"\",\n        \"description\": \"\"\n      }\n    }\n  ]\n}"
            }
          },
          "metadata": {}
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Identify categorical columns for encoding ─────────────────────────────────\n",
        "categorical_cols_to_encode = ['gender', 'company_type', 'wfh_setup_available']\n",
        "print(f\"Categorical columns to encode: {categorical_cols_to_encode}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Apply One-Hot Encoding to both datasets ───────────────────────────────────\n",
        "train_df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(train_df, columns=categorical_cols_to_encode, drop_first=True)\n",
        "test_df_encoded  = pd.get_dummies(test_df,  columns=categorical_cols_to_encode, drop_first=True)\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Align columns between train and test ──────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "# Ensure test has same dummy columns as train (fill any missing with 0)\n",
        "for col in train_df_encoded.columns:\n",
        "    if col not in test_df_encoded.columns and col != 'burn_rate':\n",
        "        test_df_encoded[col] = 0                              # add missing dummy column as 0\n",
        "\n",
        "test_cols       = [c for c in train_df_encoded.columns if c != 'burn_rate']\n",
        "test_df_encoded = test_df_encoded[test_cols]                  # align column order\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\n\\u2713 One-Hot Encoding applied.\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Shape before encoding : Train {train_df.shape}  |  Test {test_df.shape}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Shape after encoding  : Train {train_df_encoded.shape}  |  Test {test_df_encoded.shape}\")\n",
        "print(\"\\nTraining DataFrame after encoding (first 3 rows):\")\n",
        "display(train_df_encoded.head(3))\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "Qmve6XoQ8jgH"
      },
      "source": [
        "**Encoding result:**\n",
        "\n",
        "The three binary categorical columns were each replaced by a single binary dummy column:\n",
        "- `gender_Male` (1 = Male, 0 = Female)\n",
        "- `company_type_Service` (1 = Service, 0 = Product)\n",
        "- `wfh_setup_available_Yes` (1 = WFH available, 0 = No WFH)\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "8gyGno95gb_k"
      },
      "source": [
        "**✓ One-Hot Encoding applied with `drop='first'`** to eliminate the dummy variable trap. The three binary categorical features (`gender`, `company_type`, `wfh_setup_available`) each produce one binary column: `gender_Male`, `company_type_Service`, and `wfh_setup_available_Yes`. The same encoding is applied to the test set using the columns established on the training set."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "source": [
        "## 4.5 Separating Features and Target Variable\n",
        "\n",
        "We separate the feature matrix `X` from the target vector `y` before splitting into train/validation sets.\n"
      ],
      "metadata": {
        "id": "h9wQJOhHGgpm"
      }
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "source": [
        "# ── Separate features (X) and target variable (y) ────────────────────────────\n",
        "X = train_df_encoded.drop(columns=['burn_rate'])    # feature matrix — all predictors\n",
        "y = train_df_encoded['burn_rate']                    # target vector — burn rate to predict\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\u2713 Features (X) and target (y) separated.\")\n",
        "print(f\"  X shape : {X.shape}  ->  ({X.shape[0]:,} samples, {X.shape[1]} features)\")\n",
        "print(f\"  y shape : {y.shape}  ->  ({y.shape[0]:,} target values)\")\n",
        "print(f\"\\n  Feature list: {X.columns.tolist()}\")\n"
      ],
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "yiNOiox9GvET",
        "outputId": "6154065a-8552-454c-e11b-2d8bf7c23c24"
      },
      "execution_count": 43,
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ Features (X) and target (y) separated.\n",
            "  X shape : (22750, 7)  ->  (22,750 samples, 7 features)\n",
            "  y shape : (22750,)  ->  (22,750 target values)\n",

            "  Feature list: ['designation', 'resource_allocation', 'mental_fatigue_score', 'tenure_months', 'gender_Male', 'company_type_Service', 'wfh_setup_available_Yes']\n"
          ]
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "source": [
        "**Feature set confirmed:**\n",
        "\n",
        "The final feature matrix `X` contains **7 features** per employee:\n",
        "`designation`, `resource_allocation`, `mental_fatigue_score`, `tenure_months`, `gender_Male`, `company_type_Service`, `wfh_setup_available_Yes`\n"
      ],
      "metadata": {

        "**Final feature set (7 predictors):** `designation`, `resource_allocation`, `mental_fatigue_score`, `tenure_months`, `gender_Male`, `company_type_Service`, `wfh_setup_available_Yes`. Target variable `y` contains the 22,750 `burn_rate` values."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "source": [
        "## 4.6 Train-Validation Split (80% / 20%)\n",
        "\n",
        "We split the encoded data into an **80% training partition** (for model learning) and a\n",
        "**20% validation partition** (for unbiased performance evaluation). `random_state=42` ensures\n",
        "reproducibility across all runs.\n"
      ],
      "metadata": {
        "id": "fklexnbCHIpQ"
      }
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Split: 80% for model training, 20% for model validation ─────────────────\n",
        "X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(\n",
        "    X, y,\n",
        "    test_size=0.2,         # 20% held out for internal validation\n",
        "    random_state=42        # fixed seed for reproducibility\n",
        ")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\ Train-validation split complete (80% / 20%).\")\n",
        "print(f\"\\n  Training set    : X_train {X_train.shape}  |  y_train {y_train.shape}\")\n",
        "print(f\"  Validation set  : X_val   {X_val.shape}   |  y_val   {y_val.shape}\")\n",
        "print(f\"\\n  y_train mean: {y_train.mean():.4f}  |  y_val mean: {y_val.mean():.4f}\")\n",
        "print(\"  (Near-identical means confirm the split is representative of the full distribution.)\")\n"
      ],
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "N5OioRsoHb3k",
        "outputId": "4b48b9eb-26bd-4ecb-a276-95cba2c4c1cc"
      },
      "execution_count": 44,
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "\\ Train-validation split complete (80% / 20%).\n",
            "\n",
            "  Training set    : X_train (18200, 7)  |  y_train (18200,)\n",
            "  Validation set  : X_val   (4550, 7)   |  y_val   (4550,)\n",

    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "NxEr8-ufgb_q"

      "source": [
        "**✓ Train-validation split: 80% / 20%.** With `random_state=42`, the split is reproducible. This yields **18,200 training rows** and **4,550 validation rows** — sufficient for stable model evaluation. The validation set is held out completely and used only for final model assessment, not during training or tuning."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "V5r6gEdU8jgI"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 4.7 Feature Scaling — MinMaxScaler\n",
        "\n",
        "Models that use distance metrics or gradient-based optimisation (e.g., Linear Regression) are sensitive\n",
        "to the *magnitude* of feature values. Without scaling, features with large numerical ranges\n",
        "(e.g., `resource_allocation` 1–10) would dominate features with small ranges (e.g., `burn_rate` 0–1).\n",
        "\n",
        "We apply `MinMaxScaler` to transform all numerical features to the [0, 1] range.\n",
        "The scaler is **fit on training data only** — then applied to both the validation and test sets.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler\n",
        "# ── Instantiate the scaler ──────────────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "scaler = MinMaxScaler()                                            # scale to [0, 1] range\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Fit on training data only, then transform all splits ─────────────────\n",
        "X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)                    # fit + transform training set\n",
        "X_val_scaled   = scaler.transform(X_val)                          # transform validation set only\n",
        "X_test_scaled  = scaler.transform(test_df_encoded)                    # transform test set only\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Convert back to DataFrames to preserve column names ─────────────────\n",
        "feature_cols = X.columns # Define feature_cols using the columns from the original X DataFrame\n",
        "X_train_scaled = pd.DataFrame(X_train_scaled, columns=feature_cols)  # restore column names\n",
        "X_val_scaled   = pd.DataFrame(X_val_scaled,   columns=feature_cols)\n",
        "X_test_scaled  = pd.DataFrame(X_test_scaled,  columns=feature_cols)\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\u2713 MinMaxScaler fitted on X_train and applied to X_train, X_val, and test set.\")\n",
        "print(\"  All features now in the range [0, 1].\")"
      ],
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "onLt0buCI8Et",
        "outputId": "82c07d35-b8d6-419f-bdec-b040ef6a2f80"
      },
      "execution_count": 45,
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ MinMaxScaler fitted on X_train and applied to X_train, X_val, and test set.\n",
            "  All features now in the range [0, 1].\n"
          ]
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "IFlrs2x5gb_s"
      },
      "source": [
        "**✓ MinMaxScaler applied.** `MinMaxScaler` is preferred over `StandardScaler` here because `burn_rate` (the target) is a bounded [0, 1] value, and keeping features in the same [0, 1] range preserves physical interpretability. The scaler was **fit on `X_train` only** and transformed `X_train`, `X_val`, and the encoded test set — preventing test data from influencing scaling parameters (no data leakage)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "Bn2Mh99N8jgP"
      },
      "source": [
        "---\n",
        "# STEP 5: MODEL DEVELOPMENT & EVALUATION\n",
        "\n",
        "With preprocessed, split data ready, we build and evaluate predictive models.\n",
        "\n",
        "Three regression algorithms of increasing complexity are trained and benchmarked:\n",
        "1. **Linear Regression** — interpretable baseline; assumes linear relationships\n",
        "2. **Random Forest Regressor** — bagging ensemble; handles non-linearity\n",
        "3. **Gradient Boosting Regressor** — sequential boosting; typically most accurate on tabular data\n",
        "\n",
        "The best-performing model is then hyperparameter-tuned via GridSearchCV and validated\n",
        "with 5-fold cross-validation to confirm generalisation stability.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "_CVKF1VY8jgQ"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 5.1 Model Initialisation\n",
        "\n",
        "We instantiate all three models using `random_state=42` for reproducibility\n",
        "and store them in a dictionary for clean, iterable processing.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 46,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "tt7sLn4B8jgR",
        "outputId": "5c2d249d-503d-42a8-88c0-3d1abd45bdc2"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "✓ 3 regression models initialised and stored.\n",
            "  * Linear Regression\n",
            "  * Random Forest\n",
            "  * Gradient Boosting\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression\n",
        "from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, GradientBoostingRegressor\n",
        "from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, r2_score\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Initialise each model with random_state=42 for reproducibility ─────────────\n",
        "linear_reg_model     = LinearRegression()                                     # no random state needed\n",
        "random_forest_model  = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)\n",
        "gradient_boost_model = GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=0.1,\n",
        "                                                  max_depth=3, random_state=42)\n",
        "\n",
        "# ── Store models in a dictionary for clean, iterable processing ────────────────\n",
        "models = {\n",
        "    'Linear Regression':  linear_reg_model,\n",
        "    'Random Forest':      random_forest_model,\n",
        "    'Gradient Boosting':  gradient_boost_model\n",
        "}\n",
        "\n",
        "print(f\"\\u2713 {len(models)} regression models initialised and stored.\")\n",
        "for name in models:\n",
        "    print(f\"  * {name}\")\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "OrS6BEJmgb_u"
      },
      "source": [
        "**✓ Three models initialised with `random_state=42`** and stored in a dictionary for clean, iterable processing. Linear Regression serves as the interpretable baseline; Random Forest and Gradient Boosting are ensemble methods expected to capture non-linear interactions that the linear model cannot."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "VYvg_SdD8jgS"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 5.2 Model Training\n",
        "\n",
        "We train all three models on the same scaled training set to ensure a fair comparison.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 47,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "cCpmBVmw8jgT",
        "outputId": "9b185eeb-aef5-48d8-adcb-a9d4fe0a5645"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Training models on (X_train, y_train)...\n",
            "\n",

            "  ✓ Linear Regression trained successfully.\n",

      "source": [
        "print(\"Training models on (X_train, y_train)...\")\n",
        "separator = \"-\" * 45\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, model in models.items():\n",
        "    print(f\"\\n{separator}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Training: {name} ...\")\n",
        "    model.fit(X_train, y_train)                    # fit each model on the same training data\n",

        "**✓ All three models trained on `(X_train, y_train)`.** No validation data is used during training — the validation set is used only in Step 5.3 for objective comparison."
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "PJQNwDOT8jgU"
      },
      "source": [
        "## 5.3 Model Evaluation on the Validation Set\n",
        "\n",
        "We evaluate all three models on the held-out validation set using three complementary metrics:\n",
        "- **RMSE** (Root Mean Squared Error): Penalises large errors more than MAE — useful when large burnout prediction errors are particularly costly.\n",
        "- **MAE** (Mean Absolute Error): Average absolute prediction error — directly interpretable in burn rate units.\n",
        "- **R²** (Coefficient of Determination): Proportion of variance explained — 1.0 is perfect, 0.0 is no better than predicting the mean.\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "code",
      "execution_count": 48,
      "metadata": {
        "colab": {
          "base_uri": "https://localhost:8080/"
        },
        "id": "pjcui5hN8jgV",
        "outputId": "62dfbf7d-4a6b-4d76-b4c3-6fe40a9785ec"
      },
      "outputs": [
        {
          "output_type": "stream",
          "name": "stdout",
          "text": [
            "Model Evaluation on Validation Set\n",
            "\n",
            "=======================================================\n",
            "  Model: Linear Regression\n",
            "=======================================================\n",
            "  RMSE : 0.0781\n",
            "  MAE  : 0.0569\n",
            "  R2   : 0.8304\n",
            "\n",
            "=======================================================\n",
            "  Model: Random Forest\n",
            "=======================================================\n",
            "  RMSE : 0.0771\n",
            "  MAE  : 0.0568\n",
            "  R2   : 0.8347\n",
            "\n",
            "=======================================================\n",
            "  Model: Gradient Boosting\n",
            "=======================================================\n",
            "  RMSE : 0.0722\n",
            "  MAE  : 0.0534\n",
            "  R2   : 0.8554\n",
            "\n",
            "✓ All models evaluated.\n"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source": [
        "# ── Evaluate all models and store results ─────────────────────────────────────\n",
        "results   = {}\n",
        "separator = \"=\" * 55\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"Model Evaluation on Validation Set\")\n",
        "\n",
        "for name, model in models.items():\n",
        "    print(f\"\\n{separator}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  Model: {name}\")\n",
        "    print(separator)\n",
        "\n",
        "    y_pred = model.predict(X_val)                  # generate validation set predictions\n",
        "\n",
        "    rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_val, y_pred))  # root mean squared error\n",
        "    mae  = mean_absolute_error(y_val, y_pred)           # mean absolute error\n",
        "    r2   = r2_score(y_val, y_pred)                      # R-squared\n",
        "\n",
        "    results[name] = {'RMSE': rmse, 'MAE': mae, 'R2': r2, 'Predictions': y_pred}  # store metrics\n",
        "\n",
        "    print(f\"  RMSE : {rmse:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  MAE  : {mae:.4f}\")\n",
        "    print(f\"  R2   : {r2:.4f}\")\n",
        "\n",
        "print(\"\\n\\u2713 All models evaluated.\")\n"
      ]
    },
    {
      "cell_type": "markdown",
      "metadata": {
        "id": "C7nB28A18jgW"
      },
      "source": [
        "### Model Performance Comparison"
      ]
    },
